titleStmt.title: Letter from Arthur Maxwell to Edward Carson, 18 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Arthur Kenlis Maxwell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Arthur Kenlis Maxwell, 11th Baron Farnham (1879-1957) to Edward Carson (1854-1935). Carson was a prominent Unionist Politician. Originally a practicing solicitor from Dublin, Carson's opposition to the proposed third Home Rule bill made him the figure head of Unionism throughout the island. He was the centerpiece of the speaking tour that culminated in Ulster Day (28 September 1912), when just under half a million signed Ulster's solemn league and covenant pledging to use ‘all means which may be found necessary to defeat the present conspiracy to set up a home rule parliament in Dublin’. Carson inaugurated the Larne gunrunning in 1914 which armed the UVF, many of whom Carson would encourage to join the British Army throughout the First World War. Maxwell was a Unionist MP and Lieutenant Colonel of the North Irish Horse during the First World War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D1507/A/17/22 87 June 18th My dear Sir Edward, I have received a letter today
from Saunderson which fills me with concern
& misgiving. I understand from him that our
Unionist Leaders in the Cabinet knew nothing
of Loyd georges statement that the HR. question
must be settled now, that there was no
Cabinet meeting about it. And that their
opinion is that to set up a Parliament in
Dublin now, of any kind, would be pure
madness, & that any settlement which could
be arrived at, was only with a view to
getting the amending Bill passed after the
War. I am absolutely at a loss to understand
this, as our action was based on your definite
statement to the contrary. And I understood that
our Unionist leaders had directed us, & were
prepared to force HR on Ireland after the War
if a settlement was not come to now.
88 Of course if this is not the case the whole
situation is altered. And the decision of
the Ulster Council was come to under
an entirely false impression. Our Resolution was only drawn up on the understanding
that it was an Imperial necessity to settle now
an apparently this is not the opinion of
some of the Unionist Leaders in the Cabinet, &
they are opposed to any settlement now. I have incurred a very heavy Responsibility
by our action, and I am most anxious to
know the true state of affairs, as of course
if there has been a mis understanding the
situation should be cleared at once. I would be very obliged if you would
write to me at once & let me know what
has happened, as I feel very uneasy in my
mind at present. Hoping to hear from you soon
Yours sincerely
Farnham HQ 36th Division
B.E.F </p
